Control Name: Electrical Room Presence of Water Monitoring
Description: The contractor and developer will ensure that water sensors are installed in electrical rooms to provide real-time alerts in the event of water presence. These sensors will be strategically placed to detect any water accumulation, helping to mitigate risks of water damage to sensitive electrical equipment. The installation locations and minimum required quantities of water sensors will be specified in the Water Mitigation Plan, which will be maintained by the contractor and developer. This proactive approach ensures early detection and prompt response to any water-related issues, safeguarding the electrical infrastructure and enhancing overall system reliability.
Actions:
Installation - The water mitigation provider will install water sensors in electrical rooms for real-time water detection.
Placement - Sensors will be strategically placed, with location and quantity outlined in the Water Mitigation Plan.Maintenance - The Water Mitigation Plan will be maintained and reviewed to ensure effective sensor functionality.
Description Summary: Electrical Room Presence of Water Monitoring protects Electrical Rooms using presence of water monitoring.

Control Name: Suite Drains
Description: The contractor and developer will ensure the installation of drains beneath all washing machines and dishwashers to capture any leaks or overflow, preventing water from seeping into surrounding areas. This proactive measure will be incorporated into the design and construction process to reduce the risk of water damage to flooring and nearby structures. The drains will be strategically placed to provide effective water capture, helping to maintain the integrity of the surrounding areas and mitigate potential water-related risks.
Actions:
Install drains beneath washing machines and dishwashers to prevent overflow.
Integrate drain installation into overall construction and plumbing designs.
Position drains strategically to protect surrounding flooring and structures.
Description Summary: Suite Drains protects Suites using design incorporated.

Control Name: Heat Trace and Insulation
Description: The contractor will plan and install heat trace systems on all water-carrying pipes in exterior or unconditioned spaces to prevent freezing and damage. Pipes will be properly insulated using materials that meet applicable standards for thermal efficiency and durability. This ensures reliable performance and safeguards the building’s infrastructure during cold weather.
Actions:
Plan and install heat trace systems on exterior water pipes.
Insulate pipes using compliant thermal efficiency materials.
Ensure reliable performance to prevent freezing and damage.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water, Fire Suppression System, Hydronics, Temporary Water Run
Description Summary: Heat Trace and Insulation protects Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water using design incorporated.

Control Name: Pressure Reducing Valve Maintenance Plan: Safeguarding System Performance
Description: A structured maintenance plan should be implemented for pressure reducing valves (PRVs) to ensure regular inspections and timely seal replacements. Scheduled maintenance should include periodic checks of pressure and temperature gauges to detect any deviations that may indicate system issues. The plan must detail inspection intervals, replacement protocols, and necessary adjustments to optimize valve function. By systematically monitoring and maintaining these components, the risk of leaks, pressure imbalances, and system failures is significantly reduced.
Actions:
Implement – Create a structured maintenance plan for PRVs.
Inspect – Conduct regular checks of pressure, temperature, and seals.
Maintain – Schedule inspections, replacements, and adjustments to optimize valve function.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Domestic Cold Water, Fire Suppression System, Mechanical Risers, Mechanical Rooms
Description Summary: Pressure: protects Domestic Cold Water, Fire Suppression System via design incorporated.

Control Name: Proper Zoning Configuration: Optimizing Pressure Systems
Description: Separate booster pumps should be used for each pressure zone to minimize reliance on pressure-reducing valves (PRVs) and enhance system reliability. Multiple smaller pumps should be implemented instead of a single large pump to distribute workload evenly and reduce energy consumption. Proper zoning should include hydropneumatic tanks and strategic mechanical room placement, serving no more than 10 floors above and 10 floors below, to maintain stable and manageable pressure levels. Additionally, complex repressurization pump systems should be avoided to prevent excessive wear and tear.
Actions:
Use – Implement separate booster pumps for each pressure zone.
Distribute – Install multiple smaller pumps to reduce energy consumption.
Zoning – Ensure proper zoning with hydropneumatic tanks and optimal pump placement.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Domestic Cold Water, Mechanical Risers, Mechanical Rooms
Description Summary: Proper Zoning Configuration: Optimizing Pressure Systems protects Domestic Cold Water, Mechanical Risers using design incorporated.

Control Name: Floor Penetrations Water Seals
Description: Ensure and document that all floor penetrations are properly sealed to prevent water intrusion and maintain fire safety. Floor penetrations must be equipped with fire-stopping materials to prevent the spread of fire and sealed to stop water from passing through. Waterproofing membranes should be integrated with the pipe seals to maintain a continuous barrier against water ingress. By sealing the pipe penetrations before installing the insulation, the building can minimize the risk of water damage by preventing water from traveling through the insulation and potentially seeping through the floor to lower levels Non-permeable materials like calcium silicate or solid foam glass insulation should be used at floor penetrations to enhance durability and impermeability. Proper implementation of this control ensures a secure and compliant building infrastructure.
Actions:
Seal – Ensure all floor penetrations are sealed with fire-stopping materials to prevent water and fire intrusion.
Integrate – Use waterproofing membranes with pipe seals to maintain a continuous barrier against water ingress.
Use – Apply non-permeable materials like calcium silicate or solid foam glass for floor penetration insulation.
Description Summary: Floor Penetrations Water Seals protects Entire Project using design incorporated.

Control Name: Historical Project Water Incident Reports
Description: The Contractor and Developer will maintain a comprehensive record of all water-related incidents that occur during the construction and development phases, regardless of their scale or severity. This record will include detailed documentation of each event, such as the date, location, nature of the issue, corrective actions taken, and any preventative measures implemented. Once the building transitions to the operational phase, this historical data will be formally handed over to the property owner. Providing this information ensures transparency, facilitates proactive maintenance planning, and supports the property owner in managing potential water-related risks over the building’s lifecycle.
Actions:
Record – Document all water-related incidents.
Track – Log details, actions, and prevention measures.
Transfer – Handover records to the property owner.
Description Summary: Historical Project Water Incident Reports protects Entire Project using water response strategy.

Control Name: 100-Year Flood and Wind Storm Report
Description: The developer is responsible for conducting a comprehensive flood and wind risk assessment as part of the site feasibility study. This process includes obtaining detailed reports, such as a 100-year flood report and a windstorm risk analysis, to evaluate the environmental and climate-related vulnerabilities of the site. The flood risk assessment identifies the likelihood and potential severity of flooding events based on historical data and predictive modeling. Similarly, the windstorm report assesses the site's exposure to high wind events, considering factors such as regional weather patterns, topography, and past storm occurrences.
Actions:
Assess – Conduct flood and wind risk evaluations.
Analyze – Review reports on historical and predicted risks.
Document – Integrate findings into site feasibility studies.
Description Summary: 100-Year Flood and Wind Storm Report protects Entire Project using water response strategy.

Control Name: Water Mitigation Equipment Acceptance Test
Description: The contractor or the developer will accompany the water mitigation provider in a functional acceptance test of the entire water mitigation equipment installed and will sign a report attesting that all equipment is working correctly, having reliable and secure power and reliable radio, cellular or cable connectivity. Tests include in situ mechanical tests of all shutoff valves and spill tests on presence of water sensors. Any system expansion will necessarily have to also go through an acceptance test. An acceptance test report will be generated by the water mitigation provider and signed by the contractor or the developer.
Actions:
Test – Conduct a functional acceptance test with the water mitigation provider.
Verify – Ensure equipment functions correctly with secure power and connectivity.
Sign – Review and sign the acceptance test report, including system expansions.
Description Summary: Water Mitigation Equipment Acceptance Test protects Entire Project using process inspections and documentation.

Control Name: Installation Integrity: Joints, Bolts, and Piping
Description: Rigorous construction inspections must be conducted at every stage of installation to ensure proper joint connections, bolt torque settings, and material selection. Trained personnel should verify joint connections and check bolt torque to prevent errors. Clear guidelines for material specifications and selection must be enforced to ensure compatibility with project requirements. Frequent audits and supervision by qualified field engineers should be prioritized to identify potential installation issues before they escalate.
Actions:
Inspect – Conduct rigorous inspections at each installation stage for joint connections and bolt torque.
Verify – Ensure trained personnel check material compatibility and specifications.Audit – Prioritize frequent audits and supervision by qualified engineers to catch potential issues early.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water, Fire Suppression System, Hydronics, Main City Water Supply, Mechanical Risers, Mechanical Rooms, Suites, Sump Pits, Temporary Water Run
Description Summary: Installation: protects Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water via process inspections and documentation.

Control Name: Additional Fill Tests: Ensuring Water System Integrity
Description: Additional fill tests should be conducted by gradually introducing water into the system to ensure all pipes are completely filled and functional tests are performed under realistic operating conditions. The system should be filled slowly, flushed methodically, and controlled with a single pump to avoid excessive thrust forces. Starting all pumps simultaneously should be avoided to prevent water hammer and system instability. Clear and detailed startup protocols must be established and rigorously followed, with regular inspections during and after these procedures to identify hidden vulnerabilities.
Actions:
Test – Gradually introduce water into the system and perform functional tests under realistic conditions.
Control – Fill and flush the system slowly, using a single pump to avoid excessive thrust forces.
Inspect – Establish clear startup protocols and conduct regular inspections to identify potential issues.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water, Fire Suppression System, Hydronics, Main City Water Supply, Mechanical Risers, Mechanical Rooms, Suites, Temporary Water Run
Description Summary: Additional: protects Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water via process inspections and documentation.

Description Summary: Air Pressure or Water Tests in Plumbing System protects Domestic Cold Water, Domestic Hot Water using tests expansions and maintenance.

Control Name: Temporary Enclosures Plan
Description: The contractor and the developer will periodically review and maintain records of a comprehensive plan to address construction activities during inclement weather. This plan will outline specific measures to protect vulnerable elements of the construction site, such as electrical risers, from weather-related damage. As part of this plan, wooden huts or weatherproof enclosures will be constructed around electrical risers to shield them from rain, snow, or extreme temperatures. The huts will be equipped with proper ventilation and insulation to prevent moisture buildup and electrical hazards.
Actions:
Review – Periodically update the plan for inclement weather protection.
Protect – Have ready material to rapidly build wooden huts or enclosures around sensitive areas.
Ensure – Equip huts with inclination and insulation to prevent damage.
Systems and Assets at Risk: Electrical Rooms, Elevator Pits, Entire Project, Main Electrical Risers, Mechanical Risers, Suites
Description Summary: Temporary Enclosures Plan protects Electrical Rooms, Elevator Pits using tests expansions and maintenance.
